item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agent Nyazik Niyazov

I see you there.

A very Sandman-esque article. It seems like there were a few different aspects to this article that were expressed individually (caravansaray as a place of refuge against the ravages of the desert/time/destruction, caravansaray as a meeting place for different peoples, caravansaray as a sort of Sufic metaphor for idk i'm bad at poetry) but didn't really mesh together, which is a real bummer, because I think that there was something pretty transcendent, rather than just really good.

Still, anything that has two Turkmen poets who are (I think) from different tribes arguing about the relative merits of traditional vs. modern forms of identity will never merit anything but an upvote from me. Even if they decide to conclude it with one of the most replant drinks ever conceived.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I made a correction which I invite the author to evaluate.

Ah, I must go totoo.

This use of "too" makes sense in context, but it is possible that the proper correction is "I must go to [destination]" and the destination was not entered. If this is the case, please revise my correction. Thanks, and thanks for writing a very engaging scip.

item.title: Thaumiel fits for a different cause.
item.content:encoded:
I think this is thaumiel for a reason better than sadra operation. This can be a major link to future or past for getting information about dangerous events like the war against Scarlet King. Or for sending important ideas in the past to alter it in someway.
+1 multiple awesome things in a single SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since the authorship is no longer clear from this: this skip was written by me, Tufto, on my first account prior to my deleting it. You can find more of my stuff here.

item.title: Oh I love this
item.content:encoded:
This left me with chills, it transported me there so thoroughly and believably.

"Back in Soviet days" gave me a chuckle, heheh, but also wrapped my head around the time progression, the Red Shah for Scarlet King is great, the final interview is just enough without overexplaining. And i love the concept of many pasts, and many futures, especially in context of Foundation canon/'there-is-no-canon' as a whole.

It works. Everything here works, within itself AND with everything else, and it does so while telling a beautiful tale that hints of a thousand more.

Very enthusiastically upvoted. :3

Would really like to see this featured, at some point!

item.title: About that Aman
item.content:encoded:
Hi there

I am one of your SCPs' reader and I'm also a person who translate your files into Chinese on cn website.

Can I have a question, that you know in your SCP-3150, you put a poet called Aman, who talk with Magtymguly in interview A.

Since I can't find any information about him in English or Chinese on google, could you please give me a brief instruction about this Aman?

Thank you very much

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a great one, vaguely reminds me of one of the Sandman story arcs. Something I really like about this:

There are many futures and many pasts, my friend. Maybe the dark won’t come here.

It bugs me when skips set in/including stuff from the future seems to lock the Foundationverse into one path to the future, particularly apocalyptic ones, disregarding other options on the site. The above sentence gives you an out. If you like the Scarlet King storyline then that stuff is here, if you don't (or even if you just don't right now) then that is not the future for "your Foundation." I appreciate the option.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it! The article is well-written, the stories from the different characters are nice (though I don't think they flow as naturally as they could), and the Scarlet King storyline is always a cool touch.

However, as others have said, I don't really think the Thaumiel class is appropriate. Better to go with Safe, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the dialog being so fixated is well-justified within the article, since those who wind up in the caravansary leave having "solved" a conundrum. As such, it makes sense that individuals within the caravansary are fixated on that conundrum.

item.title: Re: 682 breach solution?
item.content:encoded: Ah- my headcanon is that they could not. 3150-3's results provide a "solution", but the solution is not necessarily of the kind that is wanted. If researchers tried to use it to sort out 682, they would come out deciding that the problem is not worth trying to solve- which "solves" their obession with it, but doesn't solve the problem of the lizard's containment :p.

item.title: 682 breach solution?
item.content:encoded: I wonder if they could use this to find a solution to the "problem" of 682 ALWAYS breaching containment….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy the idea of this, the Foundation's use of it, and the… intentions of the world-building a whole hell of a lot. I've enthusiastically upvoted.

One critique for future writing is that I seem to have the opposite reaction others have had to your dialog. Every single person interviewed seems to have some Enthusiastic, and, hammy, dramatic, thing, to say!!! That's the only tone that ever appears, and the interviews intentionally just end once they've said their piece. Framing it as interviews followed by "subject became unresponsive/left" doesn't give the impression of picking up random snatches of conversation walking through, which might've been your intention, it just comes off as damn awkward. (Maybe "Encounters" or just logs might be better?)

As such, there's no natural conversation flow or identifiable individual character voice in the entire thing. Every single person has worldbuilding or a story, but every single person accomplishes this by just saying it. "We're two famous poets from different time periods, which define our styles and beliefs!" "I'm from the future here's two specific things about the future now I'm done talking!" The interview with -2 is just a longer version of this.

I love the idea behind each of these encounters, in terms of both the stated characters and the events they're trying to express, but the execution leaves something to be desired.

The only other critique is that it might help to have a footnote with the definition of caravansaray when the word first appears, because it's not a common term. That's not really essential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved this one, it was well written and the implications of it are pretty neat. Love me some Scarlet King.

I do have one, fairly major quibble with it that others have mentioned previously — I feel it does not need to be classified as Thaumiel. I of course don't mean this takes away the quality of the article, but from my understanding of the Item classes, this doesn't really need a Thaumiel rating, and could be perfectly fine within the Safe class. I only say this because of my (and I feel others) personal perceptions of Thaumiel. In my mind, Thaumiel describes something huge and immense, articles whose very existence has a major impact on human civilization as a whole (like SCP-2000 or SCP-3000). I don't really conceive of Thaumiel as a class that can be applied to any SCP that aids in the maintenance/capture of other dangerous SCPs (although admittedly I can't name one off the top of my head, I feel like there are plenty of SCPs in other classes that help the Foundation with dangerous SCPs but still deserve their class ratings). Thaumiel to me is something mind-bending and mind-fucky, not something that just helps the Foundation in some form, as cool as this is.

I may sound extremely critical from that paragraph, but honestly, I still loved this article. +1, great job. Needs more publicity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know what feeling I get from this article, but whatever it is I love it. Calling the Scarlet King the Red Shah was a nice touch (and is now my favorite name for him), and there are some cool implications as to who -2 is/was.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the interviews are nicely done and well executed, the excuse that made this article Thaumiel feels too tacked on and it does not really come into play for the article. In fact, the Sadra Initiative's absence will likely not really change the interviews and words of the people inside.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only problem I have with this is, I wanted to write this exact thing but in the form of a small town in the American Southwest. And honestly, a caravanserai that involves ORIA and the Red Shah1 is way better than my idea of a diner with really good pie and oh, by the way, the proprietor is a minor deity.

All around, awesome. GG, author.

Footnotes
1. My preferred name for him is "Crimson Crown" but Red Shah is great, btw

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really good, I really enjoyed reading it. Great concept and execution, and the dialogue is well-done and doesn't appear forced. I also like the reference to the Scarlet King.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better, that's definitely a good choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Always happy to help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has a very strong sense of culture, as much as time, or place. I rather like the plethora of people you populated the interviews with. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because… er… because… look, a distraction from this plot hole!

Your post did give me the perfect solution to the problem of the picture, though- to not use a photo, but to use an old drawing instead. Shall edit accordingly :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you to eye_ball_guy for correcting this :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Spelling error here: "SCP-3150 is inhabited by a male humam"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can't say why, but I love this. +1
Only one quibble: if it's imperceptible except to those who activate it, how can they take pictures?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Sadra Initiative, as mentioned in the text- the Foundation is utilising the SCP-3150-3 effect to try to work out problems they have with containment/research. It's a mild sort of Thaumiel, but I thought that it was the most appropriate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting SCP. +1

I have one question though. Why is it Thaumiel? Perhaps I am missing something but it doesn't really aid the Foundation in locating or containing other SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My third SCP. Many thanks to Dr_Hexagon, BladeEmbrace and No0n3Left in the chat, as well as LadyKatie in the forum for their feedback.

EDIT: Oh, almost forgot- the picture is from here. Creative commons license. Although I'll be editing as soon as I can find a commons-license picture that looks more like a Safavid caravanserai, rather than a religious school…

EDIT AGAIN: I have altered the image used. It is now from here, and is public domain.
